records.ety: [OE. disese, OF. desaise; des- (L. dis-) + aise ease. See Ease.]

defs: Lack of ease; uneasiness; trouble; vexation; disquiet.
quotes: So all that night they passed in great disease.
To shield thee from diseases of the world.

defs: An alteration in the state of the body or of some of its organs, interrupting or disturbing the performance of the vital functions, and causing or threatening pain and weakness; malady; affection; illness; sickness; disorder; -- applied figuratively to the mind, to the moral character and habits, to institutions, the state, etc.
quotes: Diseases desperate grown, By desperate appliances are relieved.
The instability, injustice, and confusion introduced into the public counsels have, in truth, been the mortal diseases under which popular governments have every where perished.
synonyms: Affection. Disease is the leading medical term. Disorder mean<?
with perhaps some slight reference to an irregularity of the system. Distemper is now used by physicians only of the diseases of animals. Malady is not a medical term
and is less used than formerly in literature. Affection has special reference to the part
his disease is an affection of the lungs. A disease is usually deep-seated and permanent
malady has less of a technical sense than the other terms
and refers more especially to the suffering endured. In a figurative sense we speak of a disease mind

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Diseased (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Diseasing.]

defs: To deprive of ease; to disquiet; to trouble; to distress.
quotes: His double burden did him sore disease.

defs: To derange the vital functions of; to afflict with disease or sickness; to disorder; -- used almost exclusively in the participle diseased.
quotes: He was diseased in body and mind.
